title: Interwar foreign policy
description: Test your knowledge on foreign policy from 1920 to 1940!

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2931e6435e2009c11890c0d2c73e80c7.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “The Old Man of the Sea,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb32090397), 1941

**The policy suggested in the image was most directly a response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: concerns about American involvement in international conflicts
demands for the United States to support the Allies
fears that fascism would spread if Germany won
beliefs that America needed to serve as a peace-keeping force

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2931e6435e2009c11890c0d2c73e80c7.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “The Old Man of the Sea,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb32090397), 1941

**The sentiments expressed in the image helped prompt Congress to take which of the following actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: pass the Lend-Lease Act
closing United States ports
introduce an Embargo Act
enter World War II

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2931e6435e2009c11890c0d2c73e80c7.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “The Old Man of the Sea,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb32090397), 1941

**Which of the following best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Neutrality Act is causing the Allies to lose World War II.
The Neutrality Act is necessary to keep the US out of the war.
The Neutrality Act is stopping the recovery of the US economy.
The Neutrality Act is making American goods undesirable.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5b9a08948d44492072aa94f786a4e38c.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “It's truly encouraging how much of this stuff drifts into British ports” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/#ark:bb8908747c), 1941

**Which of the following statements is the most accurate interpretation of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Lend-Lease Act was a way for the United States to support the Allies without entering the war.
The Lend-Lease Act upheld the US policy of neutrality in the face of international conflicts.
The Lend-Lease Act prohibited the United States from sending war supplies to the Allies.
The Lend-Lease Act sent supplies to both the Axis and the Allies at equal rates to maintain neutrality.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5b9a08948d44492072aa94f786a4e38c.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “It's truly encouraging how much of this stuff drifts into British ports” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/#ark:bb8908747c), 1941

**The image was created most directly in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a new piece of legislation that allowed the United States to send aid to any of its allies
a ban on merchant ships traveling to Europe from the United States
a German and Italian military defeat and retreat from neighboring countries
a Japanese attack on American military base in Hawaii, resulting in two thousand deaths

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5b9a08948d44492072aa94f786a4e38c.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “It's truly encouraging how much of this stuff drifts into British ports” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/#ark:bb8908747c), 1941

**The image most directly illustrates a United States foreign policy that emphasized which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: providing aid to Allied countries to support the defense of the United States
maintaining true neutrality and offering aid to both the Allies and the Axis
selling goods to warring countries to help the economy recover
isolating the United States and staying out of international conflicts

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we enter the war today in order to save the British Empire, we will be involved in war for the rest of our lives. If the English Channel is our frontier, and this is our war, then we will have to defend it for years to come, for it is not a strong defensive position under the conditions of modern air warfare. In fact, to defend the Channel, we will have frequently to be undertaking military operations on the Continent of Europe. This war is not our war. We did not start it.”

>-Source: Senator Robert A. Taft, “Statement on Lend-Lease,” *The Papers of Robert A. Taft: 1939-1944,* February 26, 1941

**Which of the following best describes the key concern that the author addresses in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: demands for the United States to send aid to Allied countries who were fighting the Axis powers
lack of military training for American civilians in case the United States joins the Allies
rise of an isolationist group, aimed at keeping the United States out of World War II
complaints about the decision not to sell weapons to Europe and Asia during World War II

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we enter the war today in order to save the British Empire, we will be involved in war for the rest of our lives. If the English Channel is our frontier, and this is our war, then we will have to defend it for years to come, for it is not a strong defensive position under the conditions of modern air warfare. In fact, to defend the Channel, we will have frequently to be undertaking military operations on the Continent of Europe. This war is not our war. We did not start it.”

>-Source: Senator Robert A. Taft, “Statement on Lend-Lease,” *The Papers of Robert A. Taft: 1939-1944,* February 26, 1941

**The author’s reference to “this war is not our war” most directly reflects which of the following policy changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a policy that sent aid to Britain as their victory would also defend the United States from the Axis powers
a policy that prohibited the United States from sending war supplies to the Allies
a policy that had the United States accepting the majority of refugees from Europe and Asia
a policy that stopped the United States from creating trade relationships with warring countries

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we enter the war today in order to save the British Empire, we will be involved in war for the rest of our lives. If the English Channel is our frontier, and this is our war, then we will have to defend it for years to come, for it is not a strong defensive position under the conditions of modern air warfare. In fact, to defend the Channel, we will have frequently to be undertaking military operations on the Continent of Europe. This war is not our war. We did not start it.”

>-Source: Senator Robert A. Taft, “Statement on Lend-Lease,” *The Papers of Robert A. Taft: 1939-1944,* February 26, 1941

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the isolationist argument against US intervention
the growing interventionist mentality across the US
the response to the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor
the impact that wartime atrocities had on public support

assessment_items.question_content:
> “ Until May 1940, most Americans had viewed the war in Europe as if it were a movie— a drama that, while interesting to watch, had nothing to do with their own lives . . . By the time of Pearl Harbor, attitudes toward entering the war had shifted dramatically. According to polls, a substantial majority of the U.S. population now regarding ‘defeating Nazism’ as ‘the biggest job facing their country’; a similar majority preferred U.S. entry into the war to a German victory over Britain.”

>-Source: Lynne Olson, *Those Angry Days: Roosevelt, Lindbergh, and America’s Fight Over World War II, 1939-1941,* 2013 

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: invasions carried out by German and Japanese forces into neighboring countries
rise in Jewish refugees from Germany and Italy seeking asylum in the United States
state of the United States economy following the Great Depression
attacks on US merchant ships through unrestricted submarine warfare

assessment_items.question_content:
> “ Until May 1940, most Americans had viewed the war in Europe as if it were a movie— a drama that, while interesting to watch, had nothing to do with their own lives . . . By the time of Pearl Harbor, attitudes toward entering the war had shifted dramatically. According to polls, a substantial majority of the U.S. population now regarding ‘defeating Nazism’ as ‘the biggest job facing their country’; a similar majority preferred U.S. entry into the war to a German victory over Britain.”

>-Source: Lynne Olson, *Those Angry Days: Roosevelt, Lindbergh, and America’s Fight Over World War II, 1939-1941,* 2013 

**The conflict described in the excerpt is most similar to a conflict in what other period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The period from the 1910s to World War I
The period from the 1850s to the Civil War
The period from the 1740s to the Seven Years’ War
The period from the 2000s to the Iraqi War

assessment_items.question_content:
> “ Until May 1940, most Americans had viewed the war in Europe as if it were a movie— a drama that, while interesting to watch, had nothing to do with their own lives . . . By the time of Pearl Harbor, attitudes toward entering the war had shifted dramatically. According to polls, a substantial majority of the U.S. population now regarding ‘defeating Nazism’ as ‘the biggest job facing their country’; a similar majority preferred U.S. entry into the war to a German victory over Britain.”

>-Source: Lynne Olson, *Those Angry Days: Roosevelt, Lindbergh, and America’s Fight Over World War II, 1939-1941,* 2013 

**Which of the following statements best describes the author’s argument about public opinion before the attack on Pearl Harbor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shifted towards supporting the United States entering World War II.
It was firmly against any actions that would cause the US to enter World War II.
It supported sending aid to Allied countries, but not the US entering World War II.
It promoted an “America First” ideology and fixing the US economy.
